## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
-rw-rw-r-- 1 bhyslop bhyslop 15121 May 19 17:03 .claude/jjm/officia/260519-1000/gazette_out.md
-rw-rw-r-- 1 bhyslop bhyslop 14026 May 21 03:10 .claude/jjm/officia/260521-1003/gazette_out.md
---
## Context

Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
work, prone to silent drift.

There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
the sweep.

The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
discovery recipe, not here.

## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay

"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.

MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
inventing rationale.

Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Full target table (all in-scope enums):

| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |

Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
whole cluster.

## Scope decisions — resolved

- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
  unchanged from original framing).

## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
the candidate list and classify each hit:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
   **Migrate.**
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
   alone.**
4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
   `-tether`.

### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses

- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
  `:-bind`, etc.
- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
  stale.
- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).

### Noise ranking (classification effort)

Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
is the heavy classification grind.

## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate

Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.

**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
suites.

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
  enum missed.
- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
  tables.
- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
above.

## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)

₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
intact.

What remains and matters:
- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
  any late bugfix churn there.

## What done looks like

- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
  profiles) updated in place.
- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
  their selecting dispatch migrated.
- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.

## Out of scope

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
  by decision above.
- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:

1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
   graft cloud build.
4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
   heavy classification.
5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.

Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in

MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
inventing rationale.

Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Full target table (all in-scope enums):

| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |

Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
whole cluster.

## Scope decisions — resolved

- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
  unchanged from original framing).

## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
the candidate list and classify each hit:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
   **Migrate.**
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
   alone.**
4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
   `-tether`.

### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses

- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
  `:-bind`, etc.
- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
  stale.
- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).

### Noise ranking (classification effort)

Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
is the heavy classification grind.

## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate

Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.

**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
suites.

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
  enum missed.
- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
  tables.
- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
above.

## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)

₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
intact.

What remains and matters:
- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
  any late bugfix churn there.

## What done looks like

- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
  profiles) updated in place.
- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
  their selecting dispatch migrated.
- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.

## Out of scope

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
  by decision above.
- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:

1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
   graft cloud build.
4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
   heavy classification.
5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.

Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

file.content:
buc_require() {
  local z_prompt="$1"
  local z_required_value="$2"

  if test "${BURE_CONFIRM:-}" = "skip"; then
    buc_step "Confirm: (skipped — BURE_CONFIRM=skip)"
    return 0
  fi

  test -z "${BURE_CONFIRM:-}" || buc_die "BURE_CONFIRM must be 'skip' or unset, got '${BURE_CONFIRM}'"

  sleep 1
  printf '%b\n' "${BUC_yellow}${z_prompt}${BUC_reset}" >&2
  printf 'Type %s to confirm: ' "${z_required_value}" >&2
  local z_input
  read -r z_input </dev/tty
  test "${z_input}" = "${z_required_value}" || buc_die "Confirmation failed — expected '${z_required_value}', got '${z_input}'"
}

buc_execute() {
  set -e
  local prefix="$1"
  local title="$2"
  local env_func="$3"
  local command="${4:-}"
  shift 3; test -z "${command}" || shift

  export BUC_VERBOSE="${BUC_VERBOSE:-0}"

content.text: `buc_require` honors `BURE_CONFIRM=skip` (line 335-337), returning before the `/dev/tty` read. Re-running MZ with the documented bypass:

file.content:
rbq_qualify_fast() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running fast qualification"

  buq_tabtargets "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}" "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}" \
    "butctt.*.sh" \
    "buw-SI.*.sh" \
    "z-launcher.sh" \
    # End of exempt list
  rbq_qualify_colophons
  rbq_qualify_context
  rbrn_preflight

  buc_step "Fast qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_release() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running release qualification"

  # Phase 1: Fast qualification (tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight)
  rbq_qualify_fast

  # Phase 2: Shellcheck (expensive, release-only)
  buc_step "Running shellcheck"
  buq_shellcheck "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}" \
    "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc" \
    "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt"

  # Phase 3: Complete test suite (sequential)
  buc_step "Running complete test suite"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh"

  buc_step "Release qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_pristine() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # The gauntlet's first content fixture (pristine-lifecycle case 1) is the
  # entry-contract gate: tree clean, RBRR blank, RBRA absent, nameplate
  # hallmarks empty, vessel depot fields empty. Preceding enrollment-validation
  # is state-indifferent. The bash for-loop in rbte_suite() runs under set -e
  # so any fixture failure halts the gauntlet immediately.
  buc_step "Running pristine qualification (gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh"

  buc_step "Pristine qualification passed"
}

# eof

content.text:
The grep audit caught a real defect. `bubep_` must return nothing per acceptance, but **2 sites survive** — and one is a live regime value, not just prose:

1. `rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/bujn-winpc/burn.env:2` → `BURN_PLATFORM=bubep_windows` (live BURN node profile value)
2. `rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/README.md:17` → comment documenting `bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows`

The ₢BMAAE harmonize pace scoped its proof grep to `Tools/` (the 7 BUK files) and missed the `rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/` subtree entirely. This is exactly the "missed site" failure the docket warns about. Let me confirm it's a genuine validator-rejecting bug (not a deliberately-excluded site) before repairing — checking the enum declaration and whether other burn.env files exist.

content.text:
Now I have the full picture — **two distinct findings**, only one of which is a migration bug:

**Finding 1 — GCP project quota exhausted (environmental blocker, NOT a migration defect).** The gauntlet died at `rbtdrp_depot_stand_up` because `rbgp_depot_levy` couldn't create a depot project:
> `ERROR: Create depot project: The project cannot be created because you have exceeded your allotted project quota.`

The depot list shows **68 depots: 2 COMPLETE, 66 DELETE_REQUESTED** — leftover projects from prior gauntlet runs that haven't aged out of GCP yet. Deleted GCP projects count against your project quota for ~30 days before purge. This is an account-state blocker I can't fix from here; it needs operator action (wait for purge, manually purge, or request a quota bump). The `marshal_zero_attestation` case before it passed, so MZ and the entry contract are sound.

**Finding 2 — real missed migration site (in scope, repair obvious).** The grep audit found `bubep_` surviving in 2 places the ₢BMAAE harmonize missed because it scoped its proof grep to `Tools/` and never swept `rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/`:
- `bujn-winpc/burn.env:2` — a **live** `BURN_PLATFORM=bubep_windows`, which `burn_regime.sh:44` now validates against `bunne_*` → guaranteed rejection
- `README.md:17` — documents the stale `bubep_*` value set

Both are class-2 (live value + wire-value documentation). The gauntlet's theurge fixtures never read BURN node profiles, which is exactly why the grep audit exists as a separate gate. The repair is mechanical and obvious, so I'll fix it now.

file.content:
# BUK Node Investitures (BURN)

Per-node configuration profiles describing the shape of remote targets. Project-global, git-tracked, shared across station users.

## Structure

```
.buk/rbmn_nodes/<investiture>/burn.env
```

Each subdirectory is a BURN investiture — one node identity. The `<investiture>` is the directory name; it is not redundantly inscribed inside the file.

## Fields

```
BURN_HOST=<ip-or-hostname>       # IP address or hostname of the remote node
BURN_PLATFORM=<platform>         # bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows
```

## Git Safety

BURN profiles carry no key material — they describe node shape only. Safe to commit; shared across station users.

## Validation

```bash
tt/buw-rnl.ListNodeRegime.sh                  # all investitures
tt/buw-rnv.ValidateNodeRegime.sh <investiture>  # single investiture
tt/buw-rnr.RenderNodeRegime.sh <investiture>    # show resolved fields
```

## Companion: BURP Privileged Investitures

Privileged credentials live at `.buk/rbmu_users/<user>/<investiture>/burp.env` — per-station-user, operator-authored. Each BURP investiture name IS a investiture name by construction; the `<investiture>` directory name must match a investiture directory under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/`, enforced by file-presence check at load time.

# BUK Node Investitures (BURN)

Per-node configuration profiles describing the shape of remote targets. Project-global, git-tracked, shared across station users.

## Structure

```
.buk/rbmn_nodes/<investiture>/burn.env
```

Each subdirectory is a BURN investiture — one node identity. The `<investiture>` is the directory name; it is not redundantly inscribed inside the file.

## Fields

```
BURN_HOST=<ip-or-hostname>       # IP address or hostname of the remote node
BURN_PLATFORM=<platform>         # bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows
```

## Git Safety

BURN profiles carry no key material — they describe node shape only. Safe to commit; shared across station users.

## Validation

```bash
tt/buw-rnl.ListNodeRegime.sh                  # all investitures
tt/buw-rnv.ValidateNodeRegime.sh <investiture>  # single investiture
tt/buw-rnr.RenderNodeRegime.sh <investiture>    # show resolved fields
```

## Companion: BURP Privileged Investitures

Privileged credentials live at `.buk/rbmu_users/<user>/<investiture>/burp.env` — per-station-user, operator-authored. Each BURP investiture name IS a investiture name by construction; the `<investiture>` directory name must match a investiture directory under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/`, enforced by file-presence check at load time.

  (`BUBC_platforms_{windows,linux,mac}` holding `bubep_*`); `BURN_PLATFORM`
  re-enrolled with `bubep_linux bubep_mac bubep_windows`.

### L2. Module & Helper

- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh` BCG-compliant module per paddock.
  Sub-letter `b` = bash; legend added to BUS0 mapping section per Quoin
  Sub-Letter Discipline.
- `bujb_resolve_investiture` is sole load-and-cross-validate entrypoint,
  exposes `BUJB_RESOLVED_*` globals (set listed in paddock), single-call-
  per-process, readonly after resolution.
- Helper enforces at resolve-time: `BURP_VICEROYALTY` registered in BURN;
  key files exist with mode `0600`; privkey loads via `ssh-keygen -y` dry-
  load (proves parseable + unencrypted; replaces vague "key-pair self-
  consistency" wording — there is no second artifact, only the privkey);
  WSL default distribution = `rbtww-main`.

### L3. Tabtargets & Ceremonies

- 14 tabtargets per paddock catalog: 11 net-new + 3 modify-in-place
  (`buw-rn[lrv]` already exist, reshape for new BURN four-field shape;
  `buw-rp[lrv]` are net-new alongside `buw-jpF`, `buw-jpG[bcw]`,
  `buw-jwk`, `buw-jwc`, `buw-jws`, `buw-hj0`).
- Operational tabtargets call `bujb_resolve_investiture` first, then
  assert per-verb platform invariant inline (2-line check; helper stays
  generic): fenestrate = `bubep_windows`; garrison-b ∈ {`bubep_linux`,
  `bubep_mac`}; garrison-c/w = `bubep_windows`.
- Fenestrate ceremony per BUSJPF two-phase contract; bash orchestrator
  treats post-restart disconnect as expected phase boundary.
- Garrison ceremony per BUSJG[BCW] (workload-only, 6 steps); first admin-
  SSH failure surfaces a clear pointer to fenestrate handbook (Windows)
  or operator-managed `ssh-copy-id` (Linux/Mac).

### L4. Spec & Integration

- BUS0 / BUSJGB / BUSJGC / BUSJGW specify the three concrete `command=`
  directive strings (b/c/w) as locked spec content, sourced by the
  module rather than invented there. `command=` is a documented OpenSSH
  `authorized_keys(5)` directive; standard usage.
- BUS0 drift sweep folded in: `:burp_node:` linked term at line 127 →
  `:burp_viceroyalty:` (and any sibling stale `BURP_NODE` / `BURP_USER` /
  `BURP_SSH_PUBKEY` / `BURP_KEY_FILE` / `BURP_COMMAND` references AAp's
  sweep missed). Verify with `grep -n` before completion.
- Superseded colophons retired (audit `buw-np*`, `buw-nw*`, old
  `buw-HW*`); AAm prunes only those directly replaced by the new 14-
  target catalog. Broad sweep deferred to AAn.
- RBK Windows orchestrator (`rbhw_windows.sh`, `rbw-HWd[cdw]`,
  `rbw-h0`, `rbw-hw`) rewired to fenestrate + garrison + handbook.

### L5. Verification

- `bash -n` green on all edited shell files.
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` unchanged from baseline.

### Out of scope

PowerShell garrison + dispatch (deferred); adopt verb (deferred); user
regime (deferred); Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening verb (deferred — fenestrate
is Windows-OpenSSH only); shell-letter availability discovery on BURN
side; end-to-end operator test on a real Windows node (lives in
AAD/E/F); broad cleanup sweep (lives in AAn); operator data migration
(no migration — pre-release, nothing working to preserve).",

.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json:1255:              "jjgtn_text": "## Character

Implementation. Single pace, layered scope: Regime → Module → Tabtargets
→ Integration → Verification.

## Docket

Land the design from the heat paddock and BUS0 spec (post-AAp split:
fenestrate + garrison are sibling verbs sharing one implementation
module).

### L1. Regime & Constants

- `BURN_WORKLOADS` dropped.
- BURP restructured to four fields: `BURP_VICEROYALTY` (BURN reference),
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Privileged/workload asymmetric: USER is
  privileged-only because the workload user is project-wide convention
  (lives in BURC); KEY_FILE has both variants because each operator owns
  their own key material.
- BURP drops: `BURP_SSH_PUBKEY` (derive via `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time),
  `BURP_USER` (split), `BURP_KEY_FILE` (split), `BURP_NODE` (renamed to
  `BURP_VICEROYALTY` to match BURN's identifier vocabulary), `BURP_COMMAND`
  (moves to module per-shell-letter hardcoded mapping).
- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` added with name-collision blacklist + length
  validation.
- Encrypted privkeys rejected fast (would hang `ssh-keygen -y`).
- Prefix minted: `bub` = Bootstrap (parent non-terminal); `bube` = enum
  sub-namespace; `bubep_*` = `BURN_PLATFORM` enum values. Future enums
  follow this precedent.
- BUBC tinder constants (BCG lower_snake form):
  `BUBC_platforms_windows`, `BUBC_platforms_linux`,
  `BUBC_platforms_mac`, holding `bubep_windows`, `bubep_linux`,
  `bubep_mac`.
- `BURN_PLATFORM` enum re-enrolled with `bubep_linux bubep_mac
  bubep_windows`.

### L2. Module & Junction Helper

- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh` BCG-compliant module created.
- Module hardcodes: three shell-letter → `command=` directive mappings
  (b/c/w); workload privkey destination paths on remote per
  shell-letter; WSL distribution name `rbtww-main`; Windows OpenSSH
  `sshd_config` hardening directive set used by fenestrate.
- `bujb_resolve_investiture <investiture>` is the sole load-and-cross-
  validate entrypoint used by every operational tabtarget. Exposes
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_*` globals (readonly after resolution; single-call-
  per-process): `BUJB_RESOLVED_INVESTITURE`,
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_VICEROYALTY`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_HOST`,
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_PLATFORM`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_PRIVILEGED_USER`,
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_USER`
  (from BURC), `BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`.
- Helper enforces: `BURP_VICEROYALTY` resolves to a registered BURN
  viceroyalty; key files exist + correct perms + key-pair self-
  consistency; privkeys not encrypted.
- WSL hardcodes `rbtww-main`; mismatched current default fails fast.

### L3. Tabtargets & Ceremonies

- 14 tabtargets per paddock catalog, zipper-enrolled:
  - 6 regime config `buw-r[np][lrv]`: `buw-rn[lrv]` already exist
    (modify-in-place to reflect new BURN shape); `buw-rp[lrv]` are new.
  - `buw-jpF` (fenestrate), `buw-jpG[bcw]` (3 garrison),
    `buw-jwk`, `buw-jwc`, `buw-jws`, `buw-hj0`.
- Operational tabtargets call `bujb_resolve_investiture` first, then
  add per-verb invariants:
  - Fenestrate: platform = `bubep_windows`.
  - Garrison-b: platform ∈ {`bubep_linux`, `bubep_mac`}.
  - Garrison-c/w: platform = `bubep_windows`.
- Fenestrate ceremony works per BUSJPF two-phase contract; bash
  orchestrator treats post-restart disconnect as expected phase
  boundary.
- Garrison ceremony works per BUSJGB/C/W (workload-only, 6 steps);
  first admin-SSH failure surfaces a clear pointer to the bootstrap
  handbook procedure.

### L4. Integration

- Superseded colophons retired or reshaped (audit `buw-np*`, `buw-nw*`,
  old `buw-HW*` per cleanup-prior-attempts; AAm prunes only those
  directly replaced by the new 14-target catalog; broad sweep deferred
  to AAn).
- RBK Windows orchestrator entries (`rbhw_windows.sh`, `rbw-HWd[cdw]`,
  `rbw-h0`, `rbw-hw`) rewired to fenestrate + garrison + handbook.

### L5. Verification

- `bash -n` green on all edited shell files.
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` unchanged from baseline.

### Out of scope

PowerShell garrison + dispatch (deferred); adopt verb (deferred); user
regime (deferred); Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening verb (deferred —
fenestrate is Windows-OpenSSH only); shell-letter availability
discovery on BURN side; end-to-end operator test on a real Windows
node (lives in AAD/E/F); broad cleanup sweep (lives in AAn).",

.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json:1636:              "jjgtn_text": "## Character
Mechanical preflight mirroring the existing WSL preflight pattern.

## Docket

Locked decisions:

- Mirror zbujb_garrison_w_preflight for the b letter: at garrison entry,
  probe `sudo -n true` over admin SSH on linux/mac platforms; die with
  a diagnostic that names BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER and emits a copy-paste
  visudo line for the operator. Gate on BURN_PLATFORM in (bubep_linux,
  bubep_mac); c/w letters do not use sudo and do not need this.
- Wire via the same case-dispatch shape used for the w preflight (per
  the BCG line-1290 fix already applied) — runs before step 1 on the
  b branch.
- Paddock Standing Note: add one line declaring NOPASSWD for
  BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER as an operator-managed precondition on Linux/Mac
  hosts.

Done when:
- bash -n green on bujb_jurisdiction.sh
- tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green
- tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green at baseline
- Absent-NOPASSWD diagnostic produces a buc_die message with the visudo
  snippet in operator-readable form (verifiable by inspection)
- Paddock Standing Note for NOPASSWD-as-precondition added",
.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json:9575:              "jjgtn_text": "## Character

Design conversation. Inventory + migration plan only — no rename, no
code edits. Mount-time picks the output format and spawns subsequent
migration paces that consume the inventory.

## Goal

Produce a complete listing of regime enum values across the project
that need to be sprue-prefixed, plus the data subsequent paces need
to decide migration cadence. Convention shape was locked in the
BBABJ wrap-thread: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` — e.g.
`rbnve_conjure` for rb-vessel-regime, `bunne_linux` for bu-node-regime.
Existing `bubep_*` (BURN_PLATFORM) is the older-shape exemplar;
inventory explicitly recommends harmonize vs grandfather.

## Sweep scope — all languages

Bash, AsciiDoc specs, Python Cloud Build steps, Cloud Build YAML
substitutions, Rust test fixtures. Both declaration sites
(`buv_enum_enroll`, `buv_gate_enroll`) and every consuming use site
(equality test, case-statement arm, JSON literal, prose mention)
classified by language for cadence costing. Cadence decisions
themselves are deferred to the migration paces — this pace just
gathers the data they need.

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/{buk,rbk}/` → declarations + values
- Per discovered value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/` filtered by
  extension; cross-check regime `.adoc` specs (RBSRV, RBSRN, BUS0,
  and any other `RB[SR]*` / `BU[S]*` regime-touching spec) for prose
  mentions
- Verify no enum missed: cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations
  against the discovered enum set

## Posture

Burn-bridges migration — no backwards compatibility. Depots reform
on execution. Single-sweep target; deep testing (regime-validation,

fossil.

content.text: Good question — let me not take the rendered message at face value. The levy saved the raw LRO response JSON; that's the authoritative source. Let me read it directly.

RESP=$(find /home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/../temp-buk/temp-20260521-031228-1161559-15 -name 'rbgu_depot_project_create-poll-3s_u_resp.json' 2>/dev/null | head -1)

content.text:
Yes — it genuinely happened, and it's not a rendering artifact. The raw LRO response from GCP's Resource Manager is unambiguous:

```json
"done": true,
"error": {
  "code": 8,
  "message": "The project cannot be created because you have exceeded your allotted project quota.",
  "details": [{ "@type": ".../google.rpc.QuotaFailure", "violations": [...] }]
}
```

`code: 8` is `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` with a structured `QuotaFailure` detail — that's GCP itself refusing the create, not theurge misinterpreting something.

**On the "but I have 100 approved" puzzle** — the catch is what counts against that quota. GCP's project quota counts **active projects plus projects in `DELETE_REQUESTED` state**. Soft-deleted projects hold their slot for the full ~30-day purge window before they free up. Your `rbw-dl` showed **66 DELETE_REQUESTED** depots — those are all still consuming quota even though you've asked for their deletion.

Two more things to keep in mind:
- `rbw-dl` only lists projects matching the **depot naming patterns**. Your manor project and any other projects in the same account/billing scope also count toward the limit but won't appear in that list — so your true total is ≥ 68, possibly well past 100.
- So "approved for 100" and "quota exceeded at 68 visible depots" are fully consistent: 66 zombies + non-depot projects + the new one you're trying to create.

If you want certainty on the real number, I can run a full project count against your account using the preserved payor OAuth (the gauntlet already authenticated as Payor successfully for the `rbw-dl` step) — e.g. a `gcloud projects list` filtered by lifecycle state, including the `DELETE_REQUESTED` ones, to see exactly how close to 100 you are. Want me to do that?

94	# OAuth Authentication Functions (zrbgp_oauth_*)
95	
96	zrbgp_refresh_capture() {
97	  zrbgp_sentinel
98	
99	  # RBRO credentials already loaded and validated by caller (rbgu_rbro_load)
100	  zrbro_sentinel
101	  test -n "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID not set in environment"
102	
103	  buc_log_args "Exchanging refresh token for access token"
104	
105	  # Build request and pipe to curl - secrets never touch disk
106	  local z_response
107	  z_response=$(jq -n \
108	    --arg refresh_token "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" \
109	    --arg client_id "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" \
110	    --arg client_secret "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
111	    --arg grant_type "refresh_token" \
112	    '{
113	      refresh_token: $refresh_token,
114	      client_id: $client_id,
115	      client_secret: $client_secret,
116	      grant_type: $grant_type
117	    }' | curl -sS -X POST \
118	      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
119	      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}" \
120	      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
121	      -d @- \
122	      "${RBGC_OAUTH_TOKEN_URL}") || buc_die "Failed to execute OAuth refresh request"
123	
124	  # Check for error in response
125	  local z_error
126	  z_error=$(jq -r '.error // empty' <<<"${z_response}")
127	  if test -n "${z_error}"; then
128	    local z_error_desc
129	    z_error_desc=$(jq -r '.error_description // .error // "Unknown error"' <<<"${z_response}")

file.content:
# OAuth Authentication Functions (zrbgp_oauth_*)

zrbgp_refresh_capture() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  # RBRO credentials already loaded and validated by caller (rbgu_rbro_load)
  zrbro_sentinel
  test -n "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID not set in environment"

  buc_log_args "Exchanging refresh token for access token"

  # Build request and pipe to curl - secrets never touch disk
  local z_response
  z_response=$(jq -n \
    --arg refresh_token "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" \
    --arg client_id "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" \
    --arg client_secret "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
    --arg grant_type "refresh_token" \
    '{
      refresh_token: $refresh_token,
      client_id: $client_id,
      client_secret: $client_secret,
      grant_type: $grant_type
    }' | curl -sS -X POST \
      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      -d @- \
      "${RBGC_OAUTH_TOKEN_URL}") || buc_die "Failed to execute OAuth refresh request"

  # Check for error in response
  local z_error
  z_error=$(jq -r '.error // empty' <<<"${z_response}")
  if test -n "${z_error}"; then
    local z_error_desc
    z_error_desc=$(jq -r '.error_description // .error // "Unknown error"' <<<"${z_response}")
    buc_die "OAuth credentials expired or invalid - run rbgp_payor_oauth_refresh: ${z_error_desc}"
  fi

  local z_access_token
  z_access_token=$(jq -r '.access_token // empty' <<<"${z_response}")
  test -n "${z_access_token}" || buc_die "OAuth response missing access_token"

  echo "${z_access_token}"
}

content.text: I have all four inputs. Running the OAuth exchange and a paginated project count against the Resource Manager API, tallying by lifecycle state (secrets stay out of output):

input.command:
set +x
# Load payor credentials + client id from preserved regime
set -a
. ../station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env
. rbmm_moorings/rbrp.env
set +a

# Exchange refresh token for access token (same flow as zrbgp_refresh_capture)
TOKEN=$(jq -n \
  --arg refresh_token "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" \
  --arg client_id "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" \
  --arg client_secret "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
  --arg grant_type "refresh_token" \
  '{refresh_token:$refresh_token, client_id:$client_id, client_secret:$client_secret, grant_type:$grant_type}' \
  | curl -sS -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @- \
    "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token" | jq -r '.access_token // empty')

if test -z "${TOKEN}"; then echo "FAILED to obtain access token"; exit 1; fi
echo "Access token obtained (len ${#TOKEN})."

# Paginate projects.list, accumulate all projects into one file
: > /tmp/rbm_projects.json
PAGE=""
while : ; do
  URL="https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v1/projects?pageSize=1000"
  test -n "${PAGE}" && URL="${URL}&pageToken=${PAGE}"
  RESP=$(curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" "${URL}")
  echo "${RESP}" | jq -c '.projects[]?' >> /tmp/rbm_projects.json
  PAGE=$(echo "${RESP}" | jq -r '.nextPageToken // empty')
  test -z "${PAGE}" && break
done

echo "=== TOTAL projects visible to payor ==="
wc -l < /tmp/rbm_projects.json
echo "=== by lifecycleState ==="
jq -r '.lifecycleState' /tmp/rbm_projects.json | sort | uniq -c

**The 69 DELETE_REQUESTED are the problem, and they're not draining.** They span 2026-03-28 → 2026-05-21. GCP normally purges a deleted project ~30 days after the delete request frees its slot — but the oldest here (`rbwg-d-depot10041-260327170532`, March 28) is **54 days old and still `DELETE_REQUESTED`**. So they're stuck well past the expected purge window, not aging out on schedule.

**On your "100 approved" expectation** — I can't reconcile that to a clean "yes, you have headroom," and I won't pretend otherwise. The hard fact is GCP refused creation at 75 visible projects. Two honest possibilities:
1. The 100 increase isn't actually in effect for this principal (effective ceiling is lower than 100), **or**
2. There are ~25 more projects counting against the same account-level limit that this payor OAuth identity can't see (owned by another identity/the org).

Either way, the dominant, fixable cause is the 69 zombie deletions. This is operator/account territory — I can't purge them or change the quota from here. Practical next steps, if you want them:
- Check **IAM & Admin → Quotas** (or the project-creation request page) for your *actual* current limit — that settles the 75-vs-100 question definitively.
- Investigate why the old DELETE_REQUESTED projects aren't purging — most common cause is a **lien** on the project blocking deletion. I can list liens on the stuck ones via the API if you'd like.

No liens on any of the six oldest stuck projects — so the complaint is **justified**. The deletions aren't blocked on our side; GCP simply isn't purging them. The oldest (`rbwg-d-depot10041-260327170532`) requested deletion **2026-03-28, 54 days ago**, and GCP's documented behavior is permanent deletion ~30 days after the request. That's solid ground to stand on.

Here's your guide. I can't submit this for you — it requires your authenticated console session — but I've assembled everything you need to file it and the evidence to make it land.

## Step 1 — Pin down your *actual* current limit (settles the 75-vs-100 question)

Before requesting, confirm what you really have, so the request isn't argued on a wrong premise:

1. Console → **IAM & Admin → Quotas & System Limits** (in your manor/billing project `rbwg-p-251228075220`).
2. Filter the metric name for **"project"**. The one you hit is the **total active project limit** (the "allotted project quota"), *not* the per-minute "project creation rate" quota — don't confuse them.
3. Note the displayed limit. If it says 100, your 75 footprint shouldn't have failed — which means ~25 projects are counting from outside this identity's view, and that itself is worth raising in the ticket.

## Step 2 — File the increase to 150

The *total project count* limit is account/billing-level and usually goes through the dedicated form rather than the inline quota slider:

- Direct form: **https://support.google.com/code/contact/project_quota_increase**
- Or from the Quotas page: select the project-limit quota → **Edit Quotas / Request increase**.

Have these ready: billing account ID, the manor project ID `rbwg-p-251228075220`, and your target: **150**.

## Step 3 — Justification text (copy-paste, evidence-backed)

> **Requested new limit:** 150 active projects.
>
> **Reason for increase:** This account runs an automated release-qualification pipeline that levies and tears down ephemeral GCP depot projects on every gauntlet run. Project creation is currently being refused with `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` / "exceeded your allotted project quota."
>
> **Compounding problem — deletions are not purging within your documented 30-day window.** As of 2026-05-21 the account holds 75 projects: only **6 ACTIVE**, but **69 in `DELETE_REQUESTED`**. The oldest deletion was requested **2026-03-28 (54 days ago)** and is still pending purge. I have verified there are **no liens** on these projects blocking deletion — they should have been permanently removed at the 30-day mark per documented behavior, which would have freed the quota. Because the soft-deleted projects are not draining on schedule, they continue to consume my allotment.
>
> **What I'm asking for:** (1) raise the active-project limit to 150 to give working headroom, and (2) investigate why `DELETE_REQUESTED` projects on this account are not being purged at the 30-day mark, as this is the root cause of the exhaustion.

## What I can do alongside this

- **List the full 69 stuck project IDs + delete-request dates** as an attachment for the ticket — I have them cached; say the word and I'll dump a clean CSV.
- **Sweep liens across all 69** (I only checked the oldest 6) so you can state "zero liens, verified across all 69" with full confidence.

Which of those would help — and once you've filed it, the gauntlet re-run is still just `rbw-MZ` → `rbw-tP` whenever quota frees up.

content.text:
Hold on — before I generate that CSV, I need to correct something in my own complaint draft, because accuracy matters when you're about to point a finger at GCP support.

I cited "oldest deletion requested 2026-03-28 (54 days ago)" — but that `2026-03-28` was the project **createTime**, not the **delete-request time**. The v1 API I used doesn't expose when deletion was requested. I conflated the two, and I shouldn't let you send a pointed complaint resting on a number I can't actually substantiate. In fact, since GCP purges ~30 days *after the delete request*, anything still visible might have been deleted recently — which could mean the SLA is fine and you just have a high churn rate.

The fix: the **v3** Resource Manager API exposes `deleteTime` (actual delete-request timestamp). Let me pull that per-project so the CSV and the complaint rest on real delete-request dates — and sweep liens across all 69 at the same time.

input.command:
set +x
set -a; . ../station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env; . rbmm_moorings/rbrp.env; set +a
TOKEN=$(jq -n --arg r "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" --arg c "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" --arg s "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
  '{refresh_token:$r,client_id:$c,client_secret:$s,grant_type:"refresh_token"}' \
  | curl -sS -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @- "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token" | jq -r '.access_token // empty')
test -z "${TOKEN}" && { echo "auth failed"; exit 1; }

CSV=/tmp/rbm_stuck_projects.csv
echo "project_id,create_time,delete_request_time,days_since_delete,lien_count" > "$CSV"
NOW=$(date -u +%s)
total_liens=0; past30=0
for P in $(jq -r 'select(.lifecycleState=="DELETE_REQUESTED") | .projectId' /tmp/rbm_projects.json | sort); do
  V3=$(curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" "https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v3/projects/${P}")
  DT=$(echo "${V3}" | jq -r '.deleteTime // empty')
  CT=$(echo "${V3}" | jq -r '.createTime // empty')
  LIENS=$(curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" "https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v1/liens?parent=projects/${P}")
  NL=$(echo "${LIENS}" | jq '(.liens // []) | length')
  total_liens=$((total_liens + NL))
  if test -n "${DT}"; then
    DSEC=$(date -u -d "${DT}" +%s 2>/dev/null || echo "")
    if test -n "${DSEC}"; then DAYS=$(( (NOW - DSEC) / 86400 )); else DAYS="?"; fi
  else DAYS="(no deleteTime)"; fi
  test "${DAYS}" != "?" -a "${DAYS}" != "(no deleteTime)" && test "${DAYS}" -gt 30 2>/dev/null && past30=$((past30+1))
  echo "${P},${CT},${DT:-none},${DAYS},${NL}" >> "$CSV"
done
echo "=== CSV written: $CSV ($(wc -l < "$CSV") lines incl header) ==="
echo "=== total liens across all 69: ${total_liens} ==="
echo "=== count with >30 days since delete request: ${past30} ==="
echo; echo "=== delete-request age distribution ==="
tail -n +2 "$CSV" | awk -F, '{print $4}' | sort -n | uniq -c
echo; echo "=== oldest 8 by delete-request time ==="
tail -n +2 "$CSV" | sort -t, -k3 | head -8

The data just overturned the premise of the complaint — and I'm glad I checked before you filed it. Here's the corrected, evidence-backed picture across **all 69** stuck projects:

- **0 liens** total — confirmed across all 69, not just the oldest 6.
- **0 projects past the 30-day window.** The oldest delete *request* is **24 days ago** (`rbwg-d-depot10041-260327170532`, deleted 2026-04-26 — it was *created* March 28 but only *requested for deletion* April 26, which is why my earlier "54 days" was wrong; that was creation age).
- Every one of the 69 sits between **0 and 24 days** since its delete request. 16 were deleted today.

**So GCP's 30-day purge is not failing — it's working normally.** The root cause is purely **churn rate**: the gauntlet levies depots faster than the 30-day window clears them, so they pile up and consume quota while aging out legitimately.

That means **drop the pointed complaint** — if you send "your 30-day deletion failed," GCP support will look, see every project is within window with no liens, and the request loses credibility. The quota increase to 150 is still well justified, just on the honest grounds:

> High-churn automated qualification pipeline: ephemeral depot projects are created and deleted on every run. Soft-deleted projects correctly age out at 30 days, but run frequency creates more within any 30-day window than the current limit accommodates (currently 75 projects: 6 active, 69 legitimately pending deletion). Requesting 150 to provide working headroom for the churn rate.

content.text:
**Next batch: ~6 days from now** — but it's a trickle at first, with the real relief landing later:

| Day | Freed that day | Cumulative freed |
|-----|---------------|------------------|
| 6 | 1 | 1 |
| 7–8 | 2 | 3 |
| 13–14 | 4 | 7 |
| **15** | **9** | **16** |
| 16–22 | 11 | 27 |
| **23** | **17** | **44** |
| 24–30 | 25 | 69 |

So the timeline:
- **Day 6:** first slot frees (1 project) — barely helps.
- **Day 15:** ~16 freed — your first real headroom (a single gauntlet run levies a handful of depots).
- **Day 23:** ~44 freed — comfortable room.
- **Day 30:** all 69 cleared.

One caveat: GCP's window is documented as *"at least 30 days"* — actual purge can lag a few days past the mark, so treat these as **earliest-case** dates, not guarantees.

The practical read: if you do nothing, you're effectively quota-locked for ~6 days and only loosely workable until ~day 15. That's exactly why the increase to 150 is worth filing now — it gives you headroom immediately rather than waiting two-plus weeks for natural drainage.

content.text: Fair challenge — let me re-verify live rather than trust the snapshot from earlier in the session, and look at exactly what those 6 are.

input.command:
set +x
set -a; . ../station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env; . rbmm_moorings/rbrp.env; set +a
TOKEN=$(jq -n --arg r "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" --arg c "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" --arg s "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
  '{refresh_token:$r,client_id:$c,client_secret:$s,grant_type:"refresh_token"}' \
  | curl -sS -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @- "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token" | jq -r '.access_token // empty')
test -z "${TOKEN}" && { echo "auth failed"; exit 1; }

# FRESH live pull
: > /tmp/rbm_projects2.json
PAGE=""
while : ; do
  URL="https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v1/projects?pageSize=1000"; test -n "${PAGE}" && URL="${URL}&pageToken=${PAGE}"
  RESP=$(curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" "${URL}")
  echo "${RESP}" | jq -c '.projects[]?' >> /tmp/rbm_projects2.json
  PAGE=$(echo "${RESP}" | jq -r '.nextPageToken // empty'); test -z "${PAGE}" && break
done

echo "=== LIVE state counts ==="; jq -r '.lifecycleState' /tmp/rbm_projects2.json | sort | uniq -c
echo; echo "=== the ACTIVE ones, with detail ==="
for P in $(jq -r 'select(.lifecycleState=="ACTIVE") | .projectId' /tmp/rbm_projects2.json); do
  echo "${P}" | jq -R . >/dev/null
  jq -r --arg p "$P" 'select(.projectId==$p) | "  \(.projectId)\n    created: \(.createTime)\n    name:    \(.name)\n    parent:  \(.parent.type)/\(.parent.id)"' /tmp/rbm_projects2.json
done

content.text:
Yes — verified live, twice now: **6 ACTIVE, 69 DELETE_REQUESTED**, unchanged. But your instinct is half right. Breaking the 6 down by what they actually are tells the real story:

**Three are legitimately active — the delete-all correctly left them:**
| Project | Created | What it is |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| `rbwg-p-251228075220` | 2025-12-28 | **"Recipe Bottle Payor"** — your Manor, not a depot. A depot delete-all never touches this. |
| `cancbhl-d-canest2bhl100021` | **today** 02:11Z | **"RBGC-DEPOT"** — the canonical Linux depot (the one from your recent git log, inscribed with the reliquary). Created *after* the delete-all. |
| `cancbhm-d-canest2bhm100003` | **today** 00:38Z | **"RBGC-DEPOT"** — the canonical macOS depot. Also created today, after the delete-all. |

**Three are stragglers the delete-all appears to have MISSED — this is the real surprise:**
| Project | Created | |
|---------|---------|---|
| `prlc-d-pristq100000` | 2026-04-27 | "RB Depot pristq100000" |
| `prlc-d-pristg100000` | 2026-04-27 | "RB Depot pristg100000" |
| `prlc-d-pristg100001` | 2026-04-27 | "RB Depot pristg100001" |

These three are **24 days old and still ACTIVE, never delete-requested**. And here's the tell: your other April pristine depots — `prlc-d-pristl*`, `prlc-d-pristq100001` — *are* in DELETE_REQUESTED. So the delete-all swept the `pristl`/`pristq100001` family but skipped the `pristg100000/100001` and `pristq100000` monikers. They look like orphans the sweep's enumeration didn't catch.

So: 6 active is true, but the honest framing is **"3 expected + 3 orphans the delete-all missed."** Clearing those 3 stragglers would drop you to 3 active and is probably the cleaner near-term fix than waiting on quota.

I haven't touched them — deleting GCP projects is destructive and outward-facing, so that's your call. Want me to (a) dig into *why* the delete-all skipped the `pristg`/`pristq100000` monikers, or (b) just confirm they're safe to unmake so you can clear them?

content.text: Yes. The proper in-system path is `rbw-dU` (PayorUnmakesDepot) — it authenticates as payor and permanently removes a depot by project ID, rather than me firing raw delete calls. You've explicitly authorized these 3, so let me confirm the tabtarget's argument shape and confirmation handling, then run it on each.

=== unmake function body region ===
1268: rbgp_depot_unmake() {
1269:   zrbgp_sentinel
1270: 
1271:   # Folio arrives via BUZ_FOLIO under the param1 channel (rbz_zipper.sh
1272:   # enrolls RBZ_UNMAKE_DEPOT with channel=param1 per BBAA9). buz_exec_lookup
1273:   # extracts $1 into BUZ_FOLIO before exec'ing this function and removes it
1274:   # from $@, so reading $1 here would always be empty.
1275:   local -r z_project_id="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
1276: 
1277:   buc_doc_brief "DANGER: Permanently destroy an entire depot infrastructure"
1278:   buc_doc_param "depot_project_id" "Full GCP project ID of the depot to destroy (matches what rbw-dl displays)"
1279:   buc_doc_shown || return 0
1280: 
1281:   # Refusal ordering (cheapest first per BBAA9 docket):
1282:   #   1. Empty-arg refusal (zero traffic)
1283:   #   2. Live-disqualify (zero traffic, local string compare)
1284:   #   3. Authenticate
1285:   #   4. projects.get + state=ACTIVE + displayName discriminator
1286: 
1287:   if test -z "${z_project_id}"; then
1288:     buc_warn "rbgp_depot_unmake requires a depot project ID argument"
1289:     buc_info "Run the depot list to view candidate depots:"
1290:     buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_LIST_DEPOT}"
1291:     buc_die "Depot project ID required as first argument"
1292:   fi
1293: 
1294:   if test "${z_project_id}" = "${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}"; then
1295:     buc_warn "Refusing to unmake the live RBRD-selected depot: ${z_project_id}"
1296:     buc_info "Recovery: rename RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER in rbrd.env, or run rbw-MZ to zero regime, then retry."
1297:     buc_die "Live depot cannot be unmade — would orphan local regime state"
1298:   fi
1299: 
1300:   buc_step 'Safety confirmation required'
1301:   buc_require "DANGER: Permanently destroy depot ${z_project_id} and ALL resources" "${z_project_id}"
1302: 
1303:   buc_step 'Authenticate as Payor'
1304:   local z_token
1305:   z_token=$(zrbgp_authenticate_capture) || buc_die "Failed to authenticate as Payor via OAuth"
1306: 
1307:   buc_step 'Validate target depot'
1308:   local -r z_project_info_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects/${z_project_id}"
1309:   rbgu_http_json "GET" "${z_project_info_url}" "${z_token}" "depot_destroy_validate"
1310:   rbgu_http_require_ok "Validate depot project" "depot_destroy_validate"
1311: 
1312:   local z_lifecycle_state
1313:   z_lifecycle_state=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "depot_destroy_validate" '.state // "UNKNOWN"') || buc_die "Failed to parse project state"
1314: 
1315:   if test "${z_lifecycle_state}" != "ACTIVE"; then
1316:     if test "${z_lifecycle_state}" = "DELETE_REQUESTED"; then
1317:       buc_die "Project already marked for deletion"
1318:     else
1319:       buc_die "Project state is ${z_lifecycle_state} - can only destroy ACTIVE projects"
1320:     fi
1321:   fi
1322: 
1323:   # DisplayName discriminator — symmetric with rbgp_depot_list. Refuses any
1324:   # project whose displayName does not start with the RBGC depot anchor,
1325:   # protecting non-depot projects in the Payor's account from accidental
1326:   # destruction once unmake is decoupled from RBRR.
1327:   local z_display_name

content.text: The unmake has a key safety guard I need to check against these specific projects: a **displayName discriminator** (line 1323-1327) that refuses any project whose displayName doesn't start with the RBGC depot anchor. Notably, the 3 stragglers show displayName **"RB Depot pristg..."** while today's depots show **"RBGC-DEPOT ..."** — different prefixes. Let me read the discriminator and find the anchor constant before assuming `rbw-dU` will even accept them.

local z_display_name
  z_display_name=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "depot_destroy_validate" '.displayName // ""') || buc_die "Failed to parse displayName"
  local -r z_display_prefix="${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} "
  if [[ "${z_display_name}" != "${z_display_prefix}"* ]]; then
    buc_warn "Project displayName does not match depot anchor: ${z_display_name}"
    buc_info "Run the depot list to view candidate depots:"
    buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_LIST_DEPOT}"
    buc_die "Refusing to unmake non-depot project: ${z_project_id}"
  fi

  # Derive moniker and pool stem from the validated displayName + project_id.
  # displayName format: "${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} <moniker>"
  # project_id   format: "<cloud_prefix>${RBGC_depot_project_infix}<moniker>"
  # pool_stem    format: "<cloud_prefix><moniker>-pool" (mirrors RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM)
  local -r z_moniker="${z_display_name#${z_display_prefix}}"
  local -r z_cloud_prefix="${z_project_id%${RBGC_depot_project_infix}${z_moniker}}"
  local -r z_pool_stem="${z_cloud_prefix}${z_moniker}-pool"

  buc_step 'Clean up governor service accounts (404-tolerant)'
  # No standalone demantle verb — governor SA lifecycle ends here.  Project
  # deletion would clean these up implicitly, but an explicit DELETE pass
  # gives diagnostic visibility and makes the cleanup contract testable.
  local -r z_unmake_sa_url_base="${RBGC_API_ROOT_IAM}${RBGC_IAM_V1}/projects/${z_project_id}/serviceAccounts"
  local z_unmake_sa_page_token=""
  local z_unmake_sa_page=1
  local z_unmake_sa_url=""
  local z_unmake_sa_tok_enc=""
  local z_unmake_sa_infix=""
  local z_unmake_sa_count=0
  local z_unmake_sa_index=0

content.text:
Here's the snag — and it's the very reason these 3 survived the delete-all: **the sanctioned tool (`rbw-dU`) will refuse them.**

`rbw-dU` has a safety discriminator (line 1330) that only deletes projects whose displayName starts with the current depot anchor, `"RBGC-DEPOT"`. But these 3 were created **April 27**, before that naming existed — their displayName is the legacy `"RB Depot pristg100000"`. The guard sees a non-matching displayName and refuses, classifying them as "non-depot" projects to protect them. Your delete-all uses that same guard, which is exactly why it skipped them.

So to the question "can you?": **not through the normal tool** — it's hard-wired to refuse legacy-named depots. I *can* delete them directly via the payor API (a `projects.delete` with the same OAuth token), which bypasses that displayName guard.

I've confirmed they're genuinely safe to delete:
- Project IDs match the depot pattern (`prlc-d-pristg100000`, `-pristg100001`, `-pristq100000`)
- displayName "RB Depot ..." = legacy depot naming
- None is the live RBRD depot (RBRD was blanked by MZ)
- Deletion is GCP-soft: they go to DELETE_REQUESTED, recoverable for ~30 days

Because this means **deliberately going around the kit's own safety guard**, I want one explicit go-ahead before I do it: shall I delete those 3 directly via the payor API? (Today's two `RBGC-DEPOT` depots and your Manor stay untouched.)

message.content: yes, delete them directly via the payor API.  Thank you for your caution here, but yes we need to do this now.

input.command:
set +x
set -a; . ../station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env; . rbmm_moorings/rbrp.env; set +a
TOKEN=$(jq -n --arg r "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" --arg c "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" --arg s "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
  '{refresh_token:$r,client_id:$c,client_secret:$s,grant_type:"refresh_token"}' \
  | curl -sS -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @- "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token" | jq -r '.access_token // empty')
test -z "${TOKEN}" && { echo "auth failed"; exit 1; }

for P in prlc-d-pristg100000 prlc-d-pristg100001 prlc-d-pristq100000; do
  echo "########## DELETE ${P} ##########"
  RESP=$(curl -sS -w "\n__HTTP__%{http_code}" -X DELETE \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" \
    "https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v3/projects/${P}")
  CODE=$(echo "${RESP}" | sed -n 's/.*__HTTP__//p')
  BODY=$(echo "${RESP}" | sed 's/__HTTP__.*//')
  echo "  HTTP ${CODE}"
  echo "${BODY}" | jq -c '{name, done, "metadata.state": .metadata.state, error}' 2>/dev/null || echo "${BODY}"
done

content.text:
Done. All three confirmed `DELETE_REQUESTED`, and the account is now clean:

**3 ACTIVE** (exactly what should be live):
- `cancbhl-d-canest2bhl100021` — canonical Linux depot
- `cancbhm-d-canest2bhm100003` — canonical macOS depot
- `rbwg-p-251228075220` — your Manor

**72 DELETE_REQUESTED** (the 69 prior + the 3 just deleted), all aging out within their 30-day windows.

A few honest notes on what this changes — and doesn't:

- **Total project count is still 75**, because the 3 you just deleted moved from ACTIVE to DELETE_REQUESTED, not gone — they keep consuming quota until their ~30-day purge (they'll be the *newest* in the purge queue, ~30 days out). So this doesn't immediately unblock the gauntlet's depot levy; the quota-increase request is still the real near-term fix.
- What it *did* fix is the **straggler problem** — 3 orphaned legacy-named depots that the delete-all couldn't reach are now correctly on the deletion path, so they won't linger indefinitely as permanent quota drain.
- There's a **latent tooling gap** worth a future itch: `rbw-dU` (and the delete-all built on it) cannot reap depots created before the `RBGC-DEPOT` displayName anchor — they're invisible to the sanctioned reaper. If any more pre-April depots exist in other accounts, they'd have the same problem. Not for now, but worth capturing.

Net for the gauntlet: still gated on the GCP project quota until either the increase lands or enough of the 72 purge (first real headroom ~day 15). Whenever that clears, the re-run is `rbw-MZ` → `rbw-tP`.

message.content: Would you give me a URL to the quota upgrade?  And prepare me words that entreat an increase to 150, with a bit of a mea-culpa for still iterating on this before imminent release?

content.text:
## URL

**Project quota increase request form:**
`https://support.google.com/code/contact/project_quota_increase`

(Have your billing account ID and the manor project `rbwg-p-251228075220` on hand. If you also want to eyeball/confirm your current ceiling first: `https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/quotas?project=rbwg-p-251228075220`, filter for "project".)

## Drafted request — to 150, with a measured mea-culpa

> **Requested new project limit:** 150
>
> **Project / billing account:** `rbwg-p-251228075220` (Recipe Bottle Payor)
>
> Hello — I'd like to request an increase in my active-project limit to 150.
>
> In candor, this is largely self-inflicted: I'm in the final iteration push before a release, and my automated qualification pipeline creates and tears down ephemeral GCP depot projects on every full test run. I've been running that gauntlet more often than is tidy as I close out the last issues, and the churn has outpaced the natural 30-day deletion window.
>
> To be clear, nothing is broken on your side — I've verified my soft-deleted projects are aging out correctly (no liens, all within the 30-day window). The account currently holds 75 projects: only **3 active**, with the other **72 legitimately pending deletion**. The issue is purely that my run cadence stacks up more deletions-in-flight than my current limit accommodates, so new project creation is being refused with `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED`.
>
> A ceiling of 150 would give me the headroom to finish this release cycle without tripping the limit while the pending deletions drain. Once I'm past release, my steady-state footprint drops back to a handful of active projects. Happy to provide the full pending-deletion inventory if useful.
>
> Thank you for considering it.

A couple of things I kept deliberately out of the text, for your trust: **no claim that the 30-day deletion failed** (we verified it hasn't — that would have undercut you), and the active/pending split is stated honestly at the post-cleanup numbers (3 active / 72 pending). If you'd rather I make it terser, or drop the mea-culpa and keep it purely operational, say the word.

A ceiling of 150 gives me headroom to finish this release while the pending deletions drain. Post-release my footprint drops back to a handful. Thank you for considering it.
```
