attachment.content:
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The fresh secrets landed in `rbro.env` (mtime 17:20 today) — **but `rbrp.env` was not updated** (still 2026-05-20, still the old payor's project, billing account, and OAuth client ID). That's a problem: the OAuth refresh exchange uses `RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` + the new `RBRO_*` secrets together. If the new refresh token came from a *different* OAuth client, the old client ID in `rbrp.env` won't match it. Let me test the live exchange and see what identity/billing it actually resolves to.

input.command:
set +x
set -a; . ../station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env; . rbmm_moorings/rbrp.env; set +a
echo "Using RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID=${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}"
RESP=$(jq -n --arg r "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" --arg c "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" --arg s "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
  '{refresh_token:$r,client_id:$c,client_secret:$s,grant_type:"refresh_token"}' \
  | curl -sS -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @- "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token")
echo "=== token exchange result (error field if any) ==="
echo "${RESP}" | jq '{error, error_description, has_access_token: (has("access_token"))}'
TOKEN=$(echo "${RESP}" | jq -r '.access_token // empty')
if test -n "${TOKEN}"; then
  echo; echo "=== identity (tokeninfo) ==="
  curl -sS "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/tokeninfo?access_token=${TOKEN}" | jq '{email, scope}' 2>/dev/null
  echo; echo "=== billing accounts visible to THESE creds ==="
  curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" "https://cloudbilling.googleapis.com/v1/billingAccounts" | jq '.billingAccounts[]? | {name, displayName, open}'
fi

content.text:
This is a completely different — and properly working — setup. Big difference from before:

| | Yesterday (denied) | New payor (now) |
|---|---|---|
| Identity | bhyslop@**gmail.com** | bhyslop@**scaleinvariant.org** |
| Manor project | rbwg-p-251228075220 | rbwg-p-260521084625 (created today) |
| **Billing account** | **0173BC-6A77FA-3796BC** | **01A9C0-08DAC5-F1A2E1 ("ScaleInv")** |
| OAuth exchange | worked | ✅ works (cloud-platform + cloud-billing scopes) |

The OAuth exchange succeeds and resolves cleanly. **Crucially, the new payor is on a different, active billing account (ScaleInv) than the one that was denied** — and project-creation quota is tied to the billing account, not the OAuth client. So this should draw from a fresh pool, not inherit yesterday's 72 zombies. Let me confirm that fresh state empirically:

input.command:
set +x
set -a; . ../station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env; . rbmm_moorings/rbrp.env; set +a
TOKEN=$(jq -n --arg r "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" --arg c "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" --arg s "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
  '{refresh_token:$r,client_id:$c,client_secret:$s,grant_type:"refresh_token"}' \
  | curl -sS -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @- "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token" | jq -r '.access_token // empty')

# All projects visible to the new identity
: > /tmp/rbm_new.json; PAGE=""
while : ; do
  URL="https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v1/projects?pageSize=1000"; test -n "${PAGE}" && URL="${URL}&pageToken=${PAGE}"
  RESP=$(curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" "${URL}")
  echo "${RESP}" | jq -c '.projects[]?' >> /tmp/rbm_new.json
  PAGE=$(echo "${RESP}" | jq -r '.nextPageToken // empty'); test -z "${PAGE}" && break
done
echo "=== projects visible to new identity (scaleinvariant.org) ==="
jq -r '.lifecycleState' /tmp/rbm_new.json | sort | uniq -c
echo "--- listing ---"; jq -r '"  \(.lifecycleState)  \(.projectId)  [\(.name)]"' /tmp/rbm_new.json

echo; echo "=== projects billed to the NEW ScaleInv account (01A9C0-08DAC5-F1A2E1) ==="
N=0
for P in $(jq -r '.projectId' /tmp/rbm_new.json); do
  BA=$(curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" "https://cloudbilling.googleapis.com/v1/projects/${P}/billingInfo" | jq -r '.billingAccountName // "none"')
  echo "  ${P}: ${BA}"; N=$((N+1))
done
echo "checked ${N} projects"

content.text:
Confirmed fresh. The new payor identity sees only **5 ACTIVE projects, zero pending-deletion** — and only the manor (`rbwg-p-260521084625`) is on the new ScaleInv billing account. The others (`shopmyporchflutter*`, `centering-line`) are on entirely separate billing accounts. So there are **no zombies** in this pool; you're starting from a clean slate on both axes that matter — a new identity *and* a new billing account, both distinct from the denied `0173BC` / gmail account.

## Thoughts on the setup

It looks done and correct: OAuth exchanges cleanly with the right scopes (`cloud-platform` + `cloud-billing`), `rbrp.env` and `rbro.env` are consistent, and the manor is billed to the active ScaleInv account. Your first levy should just work on the default quota — nothing here is inheriting yesterday's exhaustion.

## On preemptively requesting an increase now — my honest counsel: don't lead with it

I'd push back gently on doing this *first*, for a concrete reason: **a brand-new billing account with one project is the weakest possible position to request a quota increase from.** GCP's reviewers look for demonstrated, legitimate usage. Yesterday they denied an account with 75 projects of *history*; a fresh account with 1 project asking for 150 is even more likely to be auto-denied or flagged as suspicious. You'd likely burn the request and have nothing to show for it.

Better sequence:

1. **Do the first levy** — prove the new payor works end-to-end (it almost certainly will).
2. **Read the actual default limit** on the new project's quota page so you know your real ceiling:
   `https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/quotas?project=rbwg-p-260521084625`
3. **Project your churn before release.** Yesterday's real root cause wasn't "limit too low" — it was the gauntlet levying a fresh depot every run while 30-day purge lagged, stacking 72 zombies. On a fresh account you start at 1. The question is: how many gauntlet runs between now and release? If it's a handful, the default likely covers you. If you'll iterate heavily, *then* request the increase — grounded in the real default + real run cadence, with the same honest "automated pipeline churn" framing that we'd already cleaned up.

## The deeper fix worth considering

If quota keeps being the bottleneck, the durable answer isn't a bigger number — it's **less churn**: have the gauntlet reuse a standing depot across runs (or expedite-delete at run end) instead of levy/unmake each cycle. That sidesteps the whole 30-day-accumulation problem regardless of the limit. Probably out of scope for the release crunch, but worth an itch.

message.content: Can you give me a prompt I can issue into a fresh claude code session on a different computer where I can use gcloud to query the actual quota you are talking about?  If indeed you think you can use gcloud to help me find the right information; it is confusing.

content.text:
Honest assessment first, since you asked: gcloud can **definitely** measure your current consumption and surface the project-creation *rate* quotas, and the modern **Cloud Quotas API** is your best shot at reading the total-project ceiling programmatically. But I want to be straight with you — there's a real chance that specific "allotted project quota" limit is an **account-level limit Google simply doesn't expose** through any API, only through the console quota page and the support form. The very fact that you can't find it in the UI is consistent with that. So the prompt below tells the other agent to try the promising routes *and* to report plainly if the ceiling turns out to be unreadable, rather than inventing a number.

Here's a self-contained prompt to paste into a fresh Claude Code session on the machine where gcloud is authenticated:

```
I need help finding a specific, confusing GCP quota using gcloud (installed and
authenticated on this machine). 

GOAL: Find the limit that controls the TOTAL NUMBER OF PROJECTS I'm allowed to
have — the one that throws this error on project creation:
  "RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED: The project cannot be created because you have exceeded
   your allotted project quota."
I find the GCP quota UI impossible to navigate and cannot locate this limit.

IDENTIFIERS:
- Identity: bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org
- Anchor project: rbwg-p-260521084625
- Billing account: 01A9C0-08DAC5-F1A2E1 ("ScaleInv") — a FRESH account
- No organization (parent type = none)

Please work through these in order and report findings at each step:

1. CONFIRM AUTH: `gcloud auth list` and `gcloud config list` — verify gcloud is
   acting as bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org.

2. MEASURE CURRENT CONSUMPTION (projects count against the limit until purged,
   ~30 days, even when DELETE_REQUESTED — and gcloud hides non-active by default):
   - `gcloud projects list --format='value(projectId,lifecycleState)'`
   - All states incl. delete-requested via the API:
     `curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $(gcloud auth print-access-token)" \
        'https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v1/projects?pageSize=1000' \
        | jq -r '.projects[] | "\(.lifecycleState)\t\(.projectId)"' | sort | uniq -c`
   - Tally how many are ACTIVE vs DELETE_REQUESTED.

3. TRY THE CLOUD QUOTAS API (modern, best shot at the actual ceiling):
     `curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $(gcloud auth print-access-token)" \
        'https://cloudquotas.googleapis.com/v1/projects/rbwg-p-260521084625/locations/global/services/cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/quotaInfos' \
        | jq .`
   Look for any quotaInfo whose name/metric mentions project COUNT or creation.
   Report the dimensions and limit values you see.

4. TRY SERVICE USAGE QUOTAS (older surface):
     `gcloud alpha services quota list \
        --service=cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com \
        --consumer=projects/rbwg-p-260521084625`
   Distinguish RATE quotas (creates per minute/day) from any TOTAL-COUNT limit.

5. HONESTY CHECK: The total active-project ceiling for a no-organization account
   is often NOT exposed via any API — only via console IAM&Admin > Quotas and the
   support form. If steps 3-4 only reveal rate quotas and no total-count limit,
   tell me that plainly. Do NOT guess or fabricate a number.

FINAL REPORT: (a) current project count by state, (b) every project-related
quota you found with its exact value, (c) a clear yes/no on whether the
total-project ceiling is discoverable via gcloud, and if not, where it actually
lives.
```

If that session comes back saying the ceiling isn't API-readable (likely), the cleanest ground truth remains the first levy itself — a fresh account creating project #2 will tell you immediately whether you have working headroom, and from there the real number to plan around is your gauntlet-run cadence before release, not the abstract limit.
